summary: Activity-oriented teams organize around functions like development, testing, and UX design, promising benefits like standardization and skill development. However, this structure causes teams to optimize for their own metrics rather than delivering useful software. It also increases batch sizes between handoffs, lengthens feedback loops, and limits team autonomy. The post argues that outcome-oriented teams with cross-functional membership are more effective for modern software development, where market responsiveness matters more than cost efficiency. Communities of practice can provide the mentoring and standardization benefits without the organizational downsides.
key_points: Activity-oriented teams optimize for their own metrics (like velocity) rather than overall software value delivery
Separating activities increases batch sizes and lengthens feedback loops between teams
Specialization itself isn't problematic—organizing teams along lines of specialization is
Market responsiveness and time-efficiency matter more than cost-efficiency in today's business climate
Communities of practice can provide mentoring and standardization without activity-oriented reporting structures
money_quotes: Activity-oriented teams are prone to optimize for their own activity and not for the bigger picture of delivering useful software.
If they are only tasked with delivering scope, they will not think about whether it is going to solve the problems it was meant to.
Specialization isn't the problem; organizing along lines of specialization is.
A separate team of testers won't accept one story at a time from a team of developers. They'd rather test a release worth of stories or at least all stories in a feature at a time.
Activity-oriented organization may be attractive from the perspective of maximizing utilization but it comes in the way of maximizing value-addition and end-to-end responsiveness.
In today's business climate, market responsiveness (time-efficiency) is more important than cost-efficiency.
key_insight: Organizing teams around activities like development or testing creates local optimization that undermines the cross-functional collaboration and fast feedback loops essential to effective software delivery.
